notes.ref: Tech Habits — The Two Lists I Rely On to Run My Day
notes.title: Tech Habits: The Two Lists I Rely On to Run My Day
notes.body:
# Tech Habits: The Two Lists I Rely On to Run My Day

## Implemented Three Ways: Paper (Bullet Journal), Kanban (Obsidian), or Calendar (Reminders + Apple Calendar)

[](https://medium.com/@geetduggal?source=post_page---byline--469ca249dc9f---------------------------------------)
Welcome to the ninth installment of Tech Habits!

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:11554/0*OpRNpf5Cys_TAkfY)

Photo by  [Maxim Tolchinskiy](https://unsplash.com/@shaikhulud?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)  on  [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

I am a  [time blocker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Timeblocking).
I use a system I call the ‘[Frontier method](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/discipline-absorbs-chaos-try-this-game-changing-simple-way-to-manage-your-time-14e262ca05bc)’ where I keep two calendars of events:

**_Sacred_**: Events I treat with the same priority as a job interview or doctor’s appointment. I will attend, no matter what.

**_Frontier_**: Events I  _intend_  to attend, roughly around this time. These are flexible and forward-looking.

Importantly, both calendars include more than just social obligations — I also block time for specific tasks, whether personal or professional.

In this post, I’ll explain how I use two core lists to guide my time blocks each day:

**_Projects_**_:_ These are the meaningful, memorable efforts I hope to complete over the coming weeks or months. They represent the “big rocks” in my day — work that moves the needle and feels worth doing.

**_Obligations_**_:_  These are the smaller, often necessary tasks. Theya re administrative items, background maintenance, and service work. They’re what keep my ship well-oiled: essential for smoothness & stability, but not necessarily the reason I set sail.

Example projects:

-   Yosemite 2025: a family trip that requires a reasonable amount of preparation and is a larger time commitment
-   Important project at work
-   Hiring for a new position
-   Backyard irrigation: hoses and timer

Example obligations:

-   Submit request for parking reimbursements
-   Help someone debug an issue they are working on
-   Get new socks
-   Help to resolve key security tickets
-   Perform a code review

The distinction between big rocks and minutiae might seem obvious, but in practice, using them alongside the Frontier method has been more powerful than I expected. Keeping both lists forces me to see, with more honesty, how much of my time is spent moving the needle on meaningful work versus how much goes toward maintenance — emails, requests, small fixes. Both matter. But without naming the types of tasks, it’s easy to blur the difference and lose track of what kind of day I’m actually having.

The real value shows up over time. By maintaining these two lists for the span of a  [season](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/atlas-journaling-a-unified-approach-for-seeing-the-big-picture-and-acting-with-focus-78804f26f5ab)  —lets say a few months — I can better align my intentions with reality. I get to ground what I hope to do against what I’ve already committed to (my  _Sacred_ calendar), and that tension helps shape more realistic plans.

More importantly, it lets me choose how seriously to treat any given day or week. If I’m falling behind on meaningful projects, I can mark time for them as  _Sacred_  instead of  _Frontier_. I don’t have to overreact in the moment because I know the season provides breathing room — but that same container also brings healthy pressure: if I want to finish what matters, I can’t drift forever.

Now imagine those two lists above in something like a Bullet Journal. You could structure your daily log / planning page like this:

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:3228/1*-zgOA4ntq7CLuqKVdqJn_Q.png)

A simple two-column approach is a convenient way to play with your day in 2D before time blocking any events. The flexibility of a ‘blank page’ allows you to take some more contextual notes and place some aspirational tasks for the day. This way you use your actual calendar for  _Sacred_  events only, for example.

My days tend to be pretty dynamic and I like to use a Kanban board in Obsidian that I call the ‘Staircase Kanban’:

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:4660/1*dFsqkpoK265vkoyFJo-12A.png)

The two core lists, Big Rocks and Minutiae, sit at the center of my system. They’re flanked by two others:  **_Idea_**  (a someday/maybe list for possible future Big Rocks) and  **_Celebrate_**  (a space to reflect on recent wins). In between sits  **_Doing_**, which anchors my focus for the current day or week.

I think of this layout as a kind of ‘staircase’, because the list sizes tend to taper from right to left. I always have far more ideas for meaningful, needle-moving projects than I have time to act on. Minutiae, or Obligations, usually outnumber the seasonal Big Rocks I actively commit to. And realistically, I can only juggle a few tasks at any one time. That’s why I maintain a ‘Doing’ list with a strict WIP (work-in-progress) limit of 3. It helps me prioritize what gets time-blocked next whether it’s a major project or just an essential but small task.

More on this idea of a ‘staircase’ or ‘funnel’ and its variants (eg. see  [Amplenote](https://www.amplenote.com/)) in an upcoming post.

Finally, one of the simplest and most practical setups I’ve found involves just using an existing task manager. I will illustrate this point using Reminders, though tools like Todoist support similar features. If you’re in the Apple ecosystem, it works seamlessly out of the box. Keep two lists (Projects and Obligations) sorted by urgency or priority. Then, literally drag and drop tasks directly into my calendar view, turning intent into scheduled time with minimal friction.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:4268/1*BtivcYALWn78Y4P1kTwabg.png)

(And if you implement a ‘Frontier’ time blocking method you can literally map specific reminders in Projects or Obligations to a Sacred or Frontier calendar.)

The real strength of the two-list approach is that it forces a clear distinction between the tasks that move the needle and the smaller, more routine obligations that keep everything running. That distinction matters. Without it, it’s easy to let the day fill up with busywork while more significant goals quietly stall. Yet by visually managing both lists in the same system, I stay grounded in reality. I am aware of what truly deserves my attention without ignoring the necessary upkeep. Best of all, it’s simple enough to implement with any of the three methods I just described, and I hope that this approach also resonates with you.

Looking forward to posting more and following-up on some points mentioned in previous Tech Habits posts soon!

’Till next time 🏔️

frontmatter.title: Tech Habits: The Two Lists I Rely On to Run My Day

notes.ref: This One Idea Changed the Way I Organized My Notes Forever
notes.title: This One Idea Changed the Way I Organized My Notes Forever
notes.body:
# This One Idea Changed the Way I Organized My Notes Forever ## _A people-first framework that works with your mind (and why Obsidian still reigns supreme)_
![1.00](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/0*NUf7cKjbrdEqk8cC.) [Geet Duggal](https://medium.com/@geetduggal?source=post_page---byline--f293371a0d5b---------------------------------------) 21 min read · Just now ![1.00](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:15000/0*R_v6f_ycDdTYW6Oh) Photo by [Tony Sebastian](https://unsplash.com/@tonyzebastian?utm_source=medium\&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium\&utm_medium=referral) Over the last year or so, I’ve been sorting my notes in a super simple way. It’s simultaneously obvious but also counter-intuitive. It’s **simple enough for almost anyone to use**, yet extensible enough to satisfy the digital world-builders amongst us. I call the method **Notable Folders**. It’s a universal principle anyone can apply, regardless of tools. Later, I’ll share **ALPS**, my personal extension that layers on task and time management, for those who want to go deeper. But the core idea stands on its own. Spoiler alert: it involves organizing around people instead of projects. And a fictional wizard helped me figure this out. More on that later. 🪄 ## People Over Projects Before I show you how this works, let me share the insight that changed the way I think about organizing notes. Years ago, I really bought into the idea that smarter search would save me from having to (over-)organize all the information I collected. But my searches kept failing. I’d waste minutes hunting for notes I knew I’d written. And somewhere deep inside, I wanted more order. So I attempted to add some order with a directory here, a (back)link there, and top it all off with some AI smarts. But I found I was still fidgety about the whole situation. What I realized was that all the hype about note-taking apps kinda misses the point. It’s not about finding things faster. _Critically_, it’s not even about what you accomplish. It’s about feeling fulfilled and content. Your notes should help _you_, the actual human using them, stay sane in a world that keeps throwing information at you. Then I came across an idea that completely changed my perspective. A famous philosopher named Immanuel Kant’s introduced a pretty convincing ‘formula for humanity’: it’s unethical to treat people, more generally conscious beings, as means to an end, rather than ends in themselves. Press enter or click to view image in full size ![1.00](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1400/0*rHG04b6C0puEZfEf.jpg) Yes, I’m citing a philosopher in an article about where to put your grocery lists, but stay with me. My realization was that all of these note-taking systems tend to emphasize goals and projects as ends in and of themselves. If you organize for actionability, projects are the central figure. And in all of these cases, the people are lost. We tend to have a bias where we neglect the people and the stories that define our projects and the spaces that we occupy. We spend a lot of time categorizing the work we do in topics and in projects, and while we may remember in our minds the people behind those stories, the representation of that in our notes is lost. And perhaps that ends up leading us to focus on the wrong things, to make the projects that we work on the ends in themselves. **The philosophy behind Notable Folders is people over projects.** Whenever you’re organizing your notes, you’re not only thinking about your projects but, perhaps even more so, thinking about _who_ is involved. And that defines how you organize things. I realized I didn’t need less structure or more structure. I needed the right kind of structure: just enough to work with my brain instead of against it. ## Three Examples of the Problem Allow me to illustrate how simple note-taking and organization can quickly become complicated and how we can simplify the situation. ## The Command Line Snippets Like many requests I receive in my job as a software engineer, I got asked a few months ago to perform a fix and do maintenance work on a part of the product I don’t touch very often. Working with a colleague, I solved the problem with a few simple incantations on the command line. I knew these might be useful later, so after I saved them to an inbox using my quick capture method, I faced a decision: where do I ultimately keep these snippets? There was no obvious one good place to put them. They weren’t generic enough for a general snippets note. If I just kept my quick note for them, I might never find it again. I could create a folder or note specific to this part of the product and name it accordingly. In the end, that might be the right move, but at the time it felt premature. To make matters worse, I knew in my deepest of hearts that despite all the search capabilities and tools out there, I still occasionally couldn’t find my useful command line snippets when I needed them. ## The Car Charger A couple of months ago I was having a brief conversation with my wife in the middle of a hectic day with the kids. She brought up whether we should get a new car charger for our electric car or figure out if there’s an adapter that works for it. It wasn’t super urgent since she already had a way to charge her car. It also wasn’t obvious that I should be the one to solve this. But something about this conversation resonated with me. Like when I worked with my collaborator on finding the command line trick, I knew something about this conversation was worth noting down. I faced the same problem: where do I put this? Beyond a simple quick capture place, I wasn’t ready to call this its own project. It might not even deserve to be a project in the first place. And it wasn’t even obvious that I should be responsible for this task. I felt like I was left with a sort of generic note with no obvious follow-up action, which kinda feels like just collecting trash. ## The Bookstore Finally, I was recently hanging out with one of my daughters at the bookstore. We browsed through different books in the kids section, and at some point, a couple of books clicked. The way I read them to her and the way she enjoyed them resonated with me. I didn’t really need or particularly want to get the books now. Moreover, we purposely avoid buying things on a whim in front of the kids to demonstrate the value of restraint and delayed gratification. But I wanted to take note of it. Maybe it was a useful gift idea? Maybe we’d get the books another time? No hard decision had been made. All I knew was that in this moment, this idea resonated and it was worth capturing. So beyond just putting it in a note that might be hard to search, I brainstormed: couldn’t I put it in a note or a list of gift ideas? Maybe the next step was talking to my wife about whether we should get the book? Again, this simple thing I wanted to capture left me feeling like I had too many choices for organizing it. ## Notable Folders You probably know where I’m going with this. In the first example, I could put this command line snippet in its own note associated with my collaborator — based on my recent interactions with him, he was becoming quickly notable in my world of software craftsmanship. In the second example, I could put the note about the car charger in a category of notes associated with my wife. In the third example, I could place these books and a quick note about what resonated between me and my daughter in a note associated with my daughter. Let’s pause for a second. In those examples, would you have intuitively thought to place these notes in a broader ‘people note’ of sorts? ‘Past me’ would have tried to categorize by some other topic or give up and submit myself to the gods of search and AI. The theme running through all of these examples is that the note resonated and were all ultimately useful, but there was no obviously good place to keep them in the moment. I now know that associating notes with people is the best way. ## What is a Notable Folder? I don’t know about you, but I feel my best ‘notes’ are in the conversations between me and others. I find myself frequently going back to a chat with someone or an email thread where I remember discussing something. Taking it further, sometimes I think messaging apps have become our best informal knowledge bases, in and out of the workplace. I find a good way to find important information is through messages I’ve _sent_ to other people as those tend to be memorable. I frequently hop into the sent section of my email or my sent messages in Slack and find what I need that way. Moreover, looking at sent messages is also a great way for me to pick up where I left off on a project. The idea behind Notable Folders is straightforward: whenever you come across a piece of information that resonates, and there’s no obvious place to put it already (eg. a project or topic of interest), take a second and consider with intent: _who_ do I associate this information most with? If this person is important in my life, or is quickly becoming notable, I’ll just create a folder for that person and start placing notes in it. Press enter or click to view image in full size ![1.00](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:2000/1*A0y4GoKbnFcpx9cprlG1wg.png) There are two key types of documents in a notable folder: **Log of notes.** If something clicks with you, **place items in their rawest form in this folder**. Videos, photos, screenshots of chat transcripts, meeting notes, command line snippets like I described above, or just ideas. The point is you quickly jot it down in whatever quick capture method you use, and when you’re processing that inbox, you sort it by default into this person’s folder if you don’t have an obviously better place for it. I call this a log because all of these notes are captured at some point in time and form the basis of a story you will be putting together. **Main document.** A clean, representative document that captures the essence of all the notes and supplementary documents in the folder. It’s the first place you visualize when you think about a notable folder. It’s your go-to document for anything related to a person of your interest. **That’s it.** If you stop reading here, and organize with this people-first philosophy, I will bet that it will be game-changing. ## Why Folders? Why not just use backlinks or tags? You certainly can. But there’s something almost primal about a container. A folder is like a box. You toss things into it. You can picture it in your mind. This is how our brains have worked since we started putting acorns in baskets. Folders are also universal. They work across every app, every operating system, every tool. When you want to feed notes into an AI or migrate to a new app five years from now, a folder full of files just works. Even if you love backlinks, you’ll want to organize/group links together in a way that often resembles folders. ## The Three Examples Revisited Here’s how the examples above actually played out using Notable Folders. (Fair warning: it’s almost anticlimactically simple.) ## The Command Line Snippets For the command line snippets, the default place where these would go is first into a simple plain text file. You can think of it as a scratch pad that I clear out or process on an approximately daily basis. Then, upon a brief moment of intentionality, a pause between the stimulus of this whole event that resonated with me and the response, I note that this collaborator is someone I’ve been working with for a while. This collaborator deserves his own space. So I place a note of command line snippets in the folder for that collaborator. Later, with my collaborator, I added more notes relating to general architecture of the system and other handy incantations. When I want to find something we discussed, I don’t search broadly. I just open his folder and find what I need. ## The Car Charger For the car charger example, I quickly created a note during the time it resonated with me and jotted down a few quick thoughts about it. Like, do we need a new charger at all? I suspect an adapter will work. In this case, it subsequently took maybe 5 to 10 minutes of research to realize that an adapter was useful. So once that got solved, it never became its own full project. However, when I was finding the right adapter I placed the candidates into a folder that represents my wife. This is an important clarification. Here’s something I did. It’s not associated with a big project, but it got completed at its own natural pace. And it’s not associated with its own space other than the person it was associated with. **And the key point is that’s okay.** The next time this comes up a year later, two years later, and I’m thinking, where did I find this charger? The easiest thing to do is recall: “I was working on this charger situation with my wife, and it was around this time.” I just quickly scan my notes around that time (or search within a folder). I’ve found that this tends to be a more reliable approach than a global search. ## The Bookstore For the final example, whenever I find a book or some product of interest, I’ll take a quick photo of it. Then using my iPhone share sheet, I’ll click on an option that saves a copy of that image into a directory. I subsequently put that file in the folder that corresponds to my daughter. No elaborate tagging system. No agonizing over categories. Just: “This moment was about my daughter, so it goes in her folder.” Done. ## Going Deeper: ALPS The nice thing about Notable Folders: it’s amenable to a lot of customization, adding the kind of color you might like to make your digital space feel like a real home. A place you’re happy to tidy up every evening and every week like you may do for your real home. What follows is my personal extension of this method — **ALPS** — which also addresses task and time management. Because those two topics are so intertwined with note-taking, I thought I would detail it here. My hope is that something in it, however minor, inspires you to tweak your workflow to be more satisfying and effective. **ALPS** stands for four categories: **Archives**, **Logs**, **Projects**, and **Spaces**. ## Logs, Projects, and Spaces: Your Active System In my system, I have three types of “active” bins: **Log (hours to days)**: A catchall bin for any note I take in the moment that’s not obviously or easily categorized. These represent my thoughts, my raw thoughts and play at any given time. Most things here will be left to the annals of history, but many items might easily fit in a current or future project or space. The purpose of the log is to always make sure I have a place to capture valuable thoughts on the scale of hours to days before they slip away. They can always be categorized later. For example, I don’t journal reflective/emotional thoughts in a physical or digital notebook. Rather, I capture voice memos almost daily. With the help of transcription and AI tools to clean them up, those journal entries often fall into my log. There are times when they tend to be more focused on projects or people and then I can place them in those notable folders. **Projects (weeks to months)**: Larger, memorable efforts that usually have well-defined deliverables or a clear idea of what the end or end date might look like. Examples could be a kitchen remodel, vacation, quarterly deliverable at work. I’m generally careful about what I call a project. At any given time, I only have a handful of actual projects. In my opinion, a well organized mind is primed for engagement and people and projects are the primary ways I engage with the world daily. For both notable people and projects, I am always most engaged if there is a next action to move things forward. **Spaces (years)**: This is where Notable Folders lives. Spaces are contexts associated with a person or an area of interest. The bar to become a space is relatively high. By default, you should place things in folders associated with people until you feel like a topic has graduated enough to become its own space. Press enter or click to view image in full size ![1.00](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:2000/1*DZFgW2Ks1-Nd1EKOo5l02w.png) You can imagine them in a sort of pyramid or ‘mountain’ where the log is at the bottom. Generally, this is the default place where you collect most items. Above that are projects: work with defined endpoints. And at the top are spaces: the enduring contexts and relationships that persist across multiple projects. When organizing for actionability, I counter-intuitively prefer **People over Projects**. In any given season of my life there are only a handful of projects I’m actively working on and it’s usually pretty obvious if a note fits neatly in one of them. What’s less obvious with any given incoming note is \_what person\_is it most actionable for? I find this to be way more practical as many topics and tasks don’t neatly fit into a big project or space you are cultivating. Rather, they are best suited for your next conversation with a person. **Special Space: Lists**. There are a number of very active lists that don’t fit neatly into a project or a space. I have found it particularly useful to simply keep all of these lists in one folder called ‘Lists’. Examples include: * Groceries * Books to read * Shows to watch * This week * … I have always found that what works best for me is to treat tasks as calendar events. In ALPS, calendar events are simply notes. I can simply turn a list item into a note with a future date and it becomes a scheduled task. You will see below that it is easy to store all of this in plain text and visualize it beautifully in Obsidian. ## Archives: Where Notable Folders Find Their Permanent Home As time progresses, you’re going to accumulate more and more notable folders, and the human mind desires some way to organize them. This is where Archives comes in. ![1.00](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:12000/0*lfjelPoRt1iKv1fd) Photo by [Loris Boulinguez](https://unsplash.com/@cli_k?utm_source=medium\&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium\&utm_medium=referral). The Long Room in Trinity College Ireland: Inspiration for the Jedi Archives I like to think of Archives more like the ‘Jedi Archives’ in Star Wars. (Yes, I’m now teasing both a potential wizard and citing a galaxy far, far away.) It’s full of valuable content that represents the most useful documents and my favorite work. It’s not just meant to simply be cold storage I’ll never revisit. It’s a place that I actually enjoy browsing. **Analogy**: think of a bookstore. When you walk in, there are tables out front with recently released books roughly grouped by category. These are meant for browsing. They’re active. That’s your Logs, Projects, and Spaces. But the not-so-prominent shelves: places you still browse, places you still want to reference quickly: that’s the Archives. The idea, just like in a bookstore, is that you want it to know by heart where the key sections are. Interestingly, even in a world full of AI and advanced searching, I’ve found that I still want a reasonable structure for my own information. I think this fundamentally has to do with the way our brains work. We tend to be very spatially oriented creatures. We remember if we put something somewhere. ![1.00](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:11424/1*EH9qMhjshg1TfhPj3hOa-Q.jpeg) Photo: My local bookstore with tables out in front ## My Archives Structure: Johnny Decimal You can organize your Archives however you like, but I use the [Johnny Decimal](https://johnnydecimal.com/) (JD) system. The beauty of Johnny Decimal is that it takes advantage of the part of our memory that associates things with specific locations. In JD, you have a fixed number of shelves (at most 10 broad areas of your life), and within each shelf, at most 10 boxes. Each folder gets a specific ID. This constraint is counterintuitively freeing. It works surprisingly well with our memory because it introduces a relatively well-balanced hierarchy. In my Notable Folders implementation, the first box in each shelf is dedicated to **notable people** in that area, and the second box to a **library** of notable books, movies, podcasts and articles. (Notable = content I have taken significant notes on). For example, here are a few shelves and boxes in my own Archives: 10-19 Life admin ... 40-49 Self-care 41 Selfish people 42 Selfish library 50-59 Spirituality 51 Spiritual people 52 Spiritual library 60-69 Software craftsmanship 61 Software people 62 Software library So my Notable Folder for Bill, when not actively in use, is tucked away here: 60-69 Software craftsmanship/ 61 Software people/ 61.14 Bill/ 62 Software library/ ... > Reasonable Person: Wait, your notable folder which probably is better titled ‘Bill/’ is now ‘61.14 Bill/’? > > Me: Yes. The numbering system helps you remember it. There’s no perfect organization system, but JD forces you to pick something and stick with it. In the last few months, if I have been unsure about whether a notable folder deserves an ID, I either figuratively trash it or let it simmer in the active Spaces directory until I’m sure it should be kept around. This creates a natural flow: Notable Folders live in your active Spaces while you’re engaging with those people, then graduate to Archives with a permanent address when the relationship stabilizes or project information is deemed notable enough to keep. ## Implementation in Obsidian I’ll now get into my implementation in Obsidian. Again, I emphasize that you can imagine creating this kind of notable folder structure using any tool of your choice. I am simply illustrating the power of Obsidian to be a very pretty and useful overlay on an otherwise very intelligible, portable, and operationally usable file structure. Let’s re-visit the command line snippet example. Here is how my workflow looked for that afternoon. I visited that person’s desk with my laptop. As we were solving the problem and I realized a few of the commands we used could be useful later, I just used my quick capture method to append lines of text to the plain text file that I call ‘Inbox’. That file looked something like this made-up example: * Bill * Handy commands for fixing product feature X fc -l | grep "\$(date +%Y-%m-%d)" du -sh ./\* | sort -h After I packed up my laptop and was heading out, I also did some quick research on toilet seats and noted down a task I needed to perform, so I used a Siri Shortcut on my phone triggered by my iPhone’s action button to quickly add text input. The file now looked like this: * Bill * Handy commands for fixing product feature X fc -l | grep "\$(date +%Y-%m-%d)" du -sh ./\* | sort -h * Toilet seat measurements: ... * Options at home depot: ... * Update 401k contributions * Milk As I was waiting for the train to arrive, I pulled out my phone again and loaded this file in Obsidian. I could just process it in plain text like above, but Obsidian can also render that exact file into a beautiful interactive Kanban board. ![1.00](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1360/1*fJh8L0UZuf8BvMVXDrFB8g.png) **(Space)** For the notes relating to Bill, I realized I didn’t have a note dedicated to Bill yet, so I tapped just a couple of times to create a new note for this card. In Obsidian, it’s trivial with another few taps to instantly turn this ‘Bill’ note into a folder using the ‘Folder Notes’ plugin and associate that folder with a calendar using the ‘Full Calendar’ plugin. I then tapped twice on the card with the command line snippet and instantly copied them to a note in the ‘Bill’ folder. You can think of the Bill folder as a sort of ‘Space’ or context where you keep a main document and notes associated with Bill. **(Project)** As I went through the next cards in the Inbox, I was similarly able to quickly copy over the toilet seat-related cards to my ‘Replace Toilet Seat’ project folder. **(Log)** Next, I quickly copied the ‘Update 401k contributions’ card to a calendar I call “Log” and drag and drop it to a time today where I’ll likely be able to get to it. I then moved the card associated with ‘Milk’ to my ‘Errands’ list and was done processing my Inbox. What’s fun about Obsidian is that the ‘Bill’ folder structure is perfectly useful in and of itself, but with the ‘Notebook Navigator’ and ‘Full Calendar’ plugins, I can visualize it with the main document associated with this person, a list of relevant notes and images in the folder, and when I took the notes on a calendar. ![1.00](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:6876/1*VKQO59LJUMSC_MaZba0Dag.png) Obsidian Screenshot. Left to right: ‘Notebook Navigator’ plugin folder view for Bill, inbox list of note, main document for Bill, note with command line snippets shown on calendar. What is particularly awesome about Obsidian and the integration with the Full Calendar plugin is that any future notes I have for Bill or any other notable folder can be treated like intended tasks to perform on my ‘frontier’ of possible events. I typically color calendar events by Project (green), Log (red), or Space (blue). ## A Well-Organized Mind This might sound ridiculous, but hear me out: my approach this type of organization was partly inspired by a fictional wizard’s thoughts on death. Yes, you read that correctly. Albus Dumbledore, the same guy who defeated dark wizards and ran a school for magic, apparently had some insights about file management. For some reason, his famous line, “To a well-organized mind, death is but the next great adventure,” kept rattling around in my head every time I stared at my digital mess. You’re thinking: “This person is about to connect mortality to where I put my grocery lists.” And you’re absolutely right. But stay with me, because somewhere between Dumbledore’s profound wisdom about life’s impermanence and my very mundane frustration with finding old meeting notes, I had a realization. Projects die. That brilliant idea you had six months ago? Dead. The detailed research you did for something that never happened? Also dead. But the people involved in those projects? They’re still here, many of them still collaborating with you, a part of new adventures. (And yes, it’s not lost on me that people also die, relationships end, and even my beloved Notable Folders system will probably seem quaint in twenty years. But compared to the lifespan of my random project ideas, people are practically immortal.) So yes, I’m essentially arguing that the secret to digital organization came to me via a children’s book character discussing the afterlife. But if you can get past the absurdity of that premise, you might find it actually makes perfect sense. When you organize around people instead of projects, you’re organizing around what endures. You’re building a system that acknowledges how life actually works: relationships persist through multiple project lifecycles, conversations spark new adventures, and the people who matter keep showing up in different contexts. That’s what a well-organized mind looks like to me. Not rigid categories or perfect taxonomies, but a structure flexible enough to embrace the ongoing adventure of being alive. Of knowing that when one chapter ends, the next one begins perhaps even with a similar cast of characters. 🏔️ ## Notable People In the spirit of this article, I want to end by acknowledging the notable people whose ideas shaped my thinking. Each deserves their own folder in my system and has a book or two I’ve gone to again and again. **David Allen.** The godfather of modern productivity. His \_Getting Things Done\_introduced the world to “mind like water” : the idea that you can only think clearly when you’ve captured everything externally into a trusted system. His emphasis on full capture, contexts, and the critical importance of defining next actions for projects fundamentally shaped how I think about engagement and moving work forward. **Cal Newport.** Author of _Deep Work_ and creator of the Time-Block Planner. Newport’s insight that we should emphasize calendars over task lists (planning _when_ we’ll do things rather than just _what_ we’ll do) revolutionized my approach to logs. His multiscale planning concept (daily, monthly, yearly) provides the temporal scaffolding for how I think about time in ALPS. **Tiago Forte.** His brilliant book _Building a Second Brain_ introduced me to the power of containers and organizing for actionability through the PARA system (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives). He also introduced me to choreographer Twyla Tharp’s concept of “project boxes” from her book _The Creative Habit:_ physical containers for collecting everything related to a creative project. This deeply influenced my thinking about folders worth noting. **Ryder Carroll.** Author of _The Bullet Journal Method_. His emphasis on intentionality (planning your time and writing notes with purpose rather than just collecting) helped me understand that the act of organizing is itself a form of reflection. The practice of migration (regularly reviewing what’s worth keeping) directly influenced how I think about graduating notes from logs to spaces. **Mark Manson.** His book _Everything is F\*cked_ dared to view productivity through a philosophical lens, introducing Kant’s categorical imperative in an accessible way. His presentation of argument that we should treat people as ends in themselves, not means to ends, is the philosophical foundation of the “people over projects” insight. (Also, I highly recommend the recently released book on Kant, called _Kant._) **Carl Pullein.** Author of _Your Time, Your Way_ and creator of the Time Sector System. His advice to be careful about what you call a project and limiting yourself to a handful at any given time has always resonated with me. His practice of always keeping a primary ‘project note’ inspired the ‘main document’ in Notable Folders. **Johnny Decimal.** Creator of the Johnny.Decimal system documented in _The Johnny.Decimal Workbook_. The elegance of using a fixed hierarchy (at most 10 areas, at most 10 categories per area) with memorable numeric IDs gave me the structure I needed for my Archives. His system takes advantage of our spatial memory in a way that finally made long-term organization feel natural. (Tip of the hat to [Ellane W](https://medium.com/u/7c1ab936a5cc?source=post_page---user_mention--f293371a0d5b---------------------------------------) or first pointing me toward JD!) **Jake Knapp & John Zeratsky.** Authors of _Make Time: How to Focus on What Matters Every Day_. Their concept of choosing a daily highlight: a single 60–90 minute focal point that will make your day feel meaningful directly influenced how I think about intent in my logs. I often create an all-day event that acts like a ‘post-it’ note to remind me of my main focus. What I love most about their approach is its playful practicality: instead of burying you in research and science, they offer over 80 concrete tactics and encourage you to run tiny personal experiments to see what works. If you’ve enjoyed this article, you might find these previous explorations fun: * [Capture To Do](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/capture-to-do-d040e1bae4a6) — This article is an evolution of that original system for managing tasks in Obsidian but extends it to note organization. * [Discipline Absorbs Chaos](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/discipline-absorbs-chaos-try-this-game-changing-simple-way-to-manage-your-time-14e262ca05bc) — Introduces the notion of a “frontier” calendar event for time management. * [Have You Been Using Your Calendar All Wrong?](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/have-you-been-using-your-calendar-all-wrong-9e686de42237) — Explores the relationship between lists and calendars. * [Build a Personal Knowledge Encyclopedia](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/want-extraordinarily-useful-and-timeless-notes-build-a-personal-knowledge-encyclopedia-bc8b7fb09829) — My first introduction to notable people and spaces, and the connection to Wikipedia-style thinking. * [Atlas Journaling](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/atlas-journaling-a-unified-approach-for-seeing-the-big-picture-and-acting-with-focus-78804f26f5ab) — With notable folders, my maps now don’t just involve projects and contexts but also people. I still love to atlas journal every season. * [Introducing the Obsidian Kanban Plus Plugin](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/introducing-the-obsidian-kanban-plus-plugin-3c970c21141a) — A practical plugin to use some of the methods I discuss here.

notes.title: Tech Habits: One Big Text File (OBTF) after 24 Hours
notes.body:
# Tech Habits: One Big Text File (OBTF) after 24 Hours

## It really is everything I hoped for … and more

[](https://medium.com/@geetduggal?source=post_page---byline--27672f999949--------------------------------)

![Geet Duggal](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:88:88/0*NUf7cKjbrdEqk8cC.)

[Geet Duggal](https://medium.com/@geetduggal?source=post_page---byline--27672f999949--------------------------------)

4 min read

·

Just now

> If it doesn’t strike a tuneful chord in you today, I’d recommend leaving it alone until it does. —  [Elanne W](https://ellanew.com/)

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1400/0*eKKiMkMnhyewOTwu)

Photo by  [ron dyar](https://unsplash.com/@prolabprints?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)  on  [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

I remember reading that in Elanne’s  [inaugural OBTF post](https://medium.com/produclivity/ptpl-091-im-using-one-big-text-file-in-obsidian-as-a-digital-bullet-journal-6f8346a3e8e0)  last year and the thought stuck with me since. I  [recently mentioned](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/tech-habits-the-right-time-to-experiment-with-task-and-capture-apps-06c775bc3aa9)  that I’ll be moving all my daily notes to a single text file from multiple text files motivated largely by the fact that “I like to  _play_  with my tasks in 2D and in the context of other contextually relevant text.” I finally got around to actually implementing it.

For the uninitiated, OBTF is a method popularized in the ‘blogosphere’  [about 20 years ago](https://danlucraft.com/blog/2008/04/plain-text-organizer/)  to keep all your digital notes in one big file,  _in plain text_  (no Microsoft Word, Apple Notes, Evernote, …).

Back then, maintaining a OBTF required a bit of manual ‘engineering’ work to properly keep data in sync. There was therefore a period where apps like Evernote had good reason to reign supreme. Their multimedia prowess and ability to work seamlessly on mobile and desktop is what made them so unique. Since then, the plain text movement has had a resurgence in part due to much better mobile apps and file synchronization support.

It sounds funny to write about OBTF after using it for just 24 hours, but the more I think about it the more it feels right. This is because I really have been thinking about this for a while. Months ago, I knew I wanted the transition to be smooth since I use so many tools around my daily log files and what I call the ‘[tick items](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/it-was-love-at-first-sight-with-this-obsidian-plugin-0616630becf7)’ in them. I therefore realized that while it is technically trivial for me to combine my individual daily log files into one big text file, my workflows for capturing data into these files as well as reading my log would have to change. (For example, I’m largely abandoning using one of my favorite Obsidian plugins ‘Thino’ in this process.)

I therefore determined a few requirements to guide me. My OBTF should be:

-   Loadable from my iOS home page  _fast_
-   Multimedia friendly
-   Easy to play around and write in the file across days (this is something that keeping plain text daily notes in their own files seems to have trouble with)
-   _Fast_  to operate in (avoid long sessions of scrolling around)
-   Friendly to use in multiple applications on both Mobile and desktop

The implementation I ultimately settled on is to slightly modify my [capture shortcut](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/the-most-useful-ios-shortcut-for-obsidian-and-beyond-revisited-9692e62c993a)  for quickly capturing media or bits of text. I use this method due to it bing an insanely fast, convenient, and plain-text friendly approach in Apple’s ecosystem. I changed my format to not include markdown task items for everything I capture. Instead it’s a single line that looks more like this and is much more pleasant to read across multiple editors:

2025-01-26 21:05 Some text or wikilink to media

I decided to use Obsidian as my preferred way to read and input data in the OBTF. It works well on Mobile and Desktop and I have it customized just slightly so it’s as much of a pleasure to read and write in it as when I code. Also  [inspired by Elanne](https://medium.com/produclivity/ptpl-115-theres-something-new-at-the-top-of-my-one-big-text-file-89d62e340fdc), The Page Scroll plugin is particularly useful on Mobile when jumping from the top and bottom of the file. I too like keeping some more persistent notes at the top.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1400/1*Kr4E8wa7W4otY0r-tsoRwA.png)

Screenshot of my OBTF in Obsidian. It doesn’t look too different in other editors aside from rendering images and other Markdown inline. I like monospaced fonts and high contrast and that is very easy to customize in Obsidian.

I did experiment with other editors including iA Writer, 1Writer, and I still use Vim and VS Code/Cursor on my desktop. I just feel like Obsidian does more of what I want with the fewest sacrifices (eg. inline media rendering, fast interaction, customizability). Obsidian is therefore my app of choice, but it’s great to know I’m not limited to Obsidian.

Some issues I noticed early-on:

-   Big files tend to be handled slowly in iA Writer on iOS and seeing other media in context is a bit of a pain. My OBTF contains nearly 25,000 lines so it might be not the best suited for certain apps.  _Solution_: use Obsidian
-   On mobile, Obsidian has a longer-than-desired initial load time.  _Solution_: optimize load time where I can
-   I found that my text was dwarfed by images making the viewing experience either too text centric (when I don’t render images), or too image-centric.  _Solution_: scale image size down using a custom CSS snippet
-   Markdown tasks make the plain text look cluttered when not rendering the markdown.  _Solution_: I prefer to just list the items one line at a time (no markdown list item) but annotated with a human readable timestamp

What I love so far

-   I  _finally_  have the free and open space to capture notes in a way that feels most natural to me.
-   I already see myself better refining and revisiting thoughts
-   Search has been more natural for me because whether it be on mobile, or my computer, I’m just using common features found in any text editor.
-   Like Elanne, I find it visually appealing to  [use special character](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/atlas-journaling-a-unified-approach-for-seeing-the-big-picture-and-acting-with-focus-78804f26f5ab)  prefixes that represent areas of cultivation for me in lines of my OBTF.

I’ll have a lot more to say about my OBTF in upcoming posts. I am quite happy it’s off to a good start despite a couple of issues early-on.

Other OBTF links:

-   [https://medium.com/@miscellaneplans/ptpl-126-what-the-dash-plus-system-looks-like-in-my-obtf-and-analog-notes-419873d27920](https://medium.com/@miscellaneplans/ptpl-126-what-the-dash-plus-system-looks-like-in-my-obtf-and-analog-notes-419873d27920)
-   [https://medium.com/produclivity/ptpl-100-one-big-text-file-top-down-or-bottom-up-b712083b3485](https://medium.com/produclivity/ptpl-100-one-big-text-file-top-down-or-bottom-up-b712083b3485)
-   [https://jeffhuang.com/productivity_text_file/](https://jeffhuang.com/productivity_text_file/)
-   [https://medium.com/@mikegrindle/my-one-big-text-file-8159fe941be3](https://medium.com/@mikegrindle/my-one-big-text-file-8159fe941be3)

Wait, no YouTube videos. No podcast? No social media?” Yup, still blogging like it’s 2005 in 2025. ‘Till next time 🏔️

notes.ref: Introducing the Obsidian Kanban Plus Plugin
notes.title: Introducing the Obsidian Kanban Plus Plugin
notes.body:
# Introducing the Obsidian Kanban Plus Plugin


----------

### Introducing the Obsidian Kanban Plus Plugin

#### A feature rich modular playground that builds on the already awesome Kanban Plugin

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*8ioJUIqbI8qKz7AK0Ah35Q.png)

Today I’m excited to announce the pre-release of the  [Kanban Plus plugin](https://github.com/geetduggal/obsidian-kanban/tree/add-copy-to-calendar-feature)! It is a fork of the very popular Obsidian Kanban Plugin. Kanban Plus is meant to serve as a place where I can play with thoughtful and potentially powerful extensions to the already excellent foundation from the Kanban plugin.

I’m releasing this because I’ve gotten so much value using these features on a day-to-day basis over the past few months. I only spent a couple of hours developing it thanks to tools like ChatGPT, so the ‘value added to time spent developing ratio’ is super high. If even one more person finds the ideas here useful, it will have made releasing it worth it!

### Begin with one file and one list

Obsidian has earned a reputation as a ‘productivity-nerd’ platform, drawing in tinkerers who get lost in endlessly adjusting their systems. While there is truth in this sentiment,  **I’m convinced that Obsidian’s greatest strengths show up when it’s used simply**.

My suggestion just start using Obsidian as an Inbox of  _stuff_. It is literally just one file. Create a heading called ‘ 📥 Inbox’ and just place text in a bulleted list.

In Obsidian this file is a simple plain text file but it is pretty powerful.

-   List items can not only be plain text, but also links to the web, links to other notes, images, audio, etc.
-   You can use Desktop and Mobile apps to add to and view this list.
-   You can create sublists and expand and collapse each of them
-   Lists can be tasks lists
-   etc.

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*X3CQLeAn82qJyDhYoBeHUw.png)

Above is an example list in Obsidian. You can see how it’s already quite a multimedia experience. Everything is in plain text, but Obsidian renders it for you as you paste images and type in plain text.

This is already quite powerful and we’re not even using the Kanban plugin yet! You can add and remove items. You can reorder them. You can visualize what is on your plate. This is already an excellent start to using Obsidian.

To turn this into a Kanban board using the Kanban plugin, you simply install the community plugin from the settings page and then add this to the top of the file:

---
kanban-plugin: basic
---

You then get a view like this image below:

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*OzIjYb6ZcUpI8R46usW5JQ.png)

Each bullet point is now a card that you can easily drag and drop in desktop and in mobile. You can delete cards, turn cards into notes to add a lot more information, and more!

You can even get advanced quick capture in a super convenient way with just one list in Obsidian. For example, if you use iOS, you can  [create a shortcut](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/the-most-useful-ios-shortcut-for-obsidian-and-beyond-revisited-9692e62c993a)  that automatically appends to this file locally on your phone and syncs to the cloud. This makes it very easy to quickly add text and media through iOS native functionality. For example, I can just ask Siri to capture some text I speak out loud, or use the ‘Share’ button in iOS on an image to append an image to the list.

I’d recommend just playing around with this one list for a week or two. It is incredibly useful to just get stuff off your mind onto a list you occasionally review and  [ruthlessly delete](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/capture-to-do-d040e1bae4a6). With Obsidian and the Kanban plugin, you essentially get full-featured free Kanban app that is Desktop and Mobile friendly. You’re not locked in to any fancy file format or other app and can manipulate the text files easily and directly if you like.

### Add complexity organically

While it may be possible to simply live off of one list in perpetuity, you may likely find yourself wanting to keep and sort some items in a lightweight way. Just add what you need incrementally and think carefully about each addition as opposed to trying to create a complicated and fancy system. Make sure your system is meaningful to you and stick with it for a bit. Here are some examples of adding complexity organically.

#### Maintain ‘📽️ Projects’ and ‘✅ Obligation’ lists

The former list is meant to contain big projects while the latter tend to be smaller obligations that you need to get done on the timescale of a season (a few weeks to a few months roughly). The Kanban plugin makes it easy to drag and drop items between these lists.

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*-kawE4DPOcStb3nhTkUK8Q.png)

#### Keep a few ‘Inspirational lists’

While projects and obligations can be thought of as tasks at different scales, inspirational lists are simply things you are interested in like quotes or books.

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*SMSxIYNQHD0dOfXZQVKVbw.png)

#### Copy list items to a calendar view / log

The first feature developed for what has now become Kanban Plus is called ‘[Copy to calendar](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/tech-habits-obsidian-kanban-and-full-calendar-integration-a05a7ff2d2f6)’. The idea here is that the lists that we keep may often be associated with events on a calendar and Kanban Plus integrates with  [Obsidian’s Full Calendar plugin](https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar).

For example, you may keep a list of things that you do approximately weekly or monthly. When you’re ready to schedule one of those items, you simply copy it over to the calendar. You can take notes on a note associated with the list item itself or the specific calendar event. This feature is useful even with just one ‘📥 Inbox’ list. For example, if there a few things you think you need to do in the next few days, just place them in your inbox and copy over to your calendar to block off some time.

This combination of lists and calendar to me is like  **the ultimate digital pocket notebook**. Rather than open an app and get distracted with all kinds of notifications, I often find myself now opening Obsidian on my phone. My lists show up front-end-center and my calendar / log of what I’m doing (either a 1 or 3 day view) is just a ‘swipe left’ away.

I discuss the relationship between calendars and lists in a bit more depth  [here](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/have-you-been-using-your-calendar-all-wrong-9e686de42237).

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/0*ZSURcFI5hXvxwE3q.gif)

#### Move items to other files

Obsidian Kanban already allows you to move items from one list to another. It is such an easy and natural process to build lists and then subsequently refine and group them in files that I thought it would be useful extend this functionality to the Kanban Plugin. With this feature, you can associate a file with a Kanban board throught he board settings page. In this case I’m going to associate the example board above with a topic called ‘Artificial Intelligence’ (see  [this article](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/want-extraordinarily-useful-and-timeless-notes-build-a-personal-knowledge-encyclopedia-bc8b7fb09829)  for how I think about topics).

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*O8HlEYDn-4hllpreyz-7qw.png)

Now, let’s consider the ‘Great article by Cal Newport’ list item above. It happens to be about AI (actually even though this is just a made-up article for this made-up board, he did write a  [noteworthy article on AI for the  _New Yorker_](https://www.newyorker.com/culture/open-questions/what-if-ai-doesnt-get-much-better-than-this)  recently). I’d like to incorporate some thoughts from this in the space that I cultivate, so I simply move this item there. Even if there are specific thoughts from the article in my Inbox, eg, I can simply move them over with a click of a mouse or a tap on the phone. And since it’s plain text, all of this works, of course, with your favorite text editor!

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*J9XoCUk8WEJAFQDIBZdfmw.png)

#### ‘List mind mapping’

Taking this concept of moving cards to topic-specific lists further, you can refine lists within a topic or a project and play with ideas much like you would with nested bulleted outlines or mind mapping. There is one key difference with a Kanban Plus approach.

It’s not so much about creating big hierarchies or graphs of related notes. It’s more about  **ordering items within a list** and**putting lists together in a specific order**. For example, consider a ‘meta’ example Kanban board (again this is just a simple markdown file with a few headings and bullet points) about Kanban Plus:

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*6fspdKt63LcS6YIR7Okfyg.png)

You can see here how I’m building the ideas up in this simple article. I can create sections/categories and order and drag-and-drop items with ease. Rather than have arbitrarily deep hierarchies or complex graphs, Kanban Plus allows you to spatially organize your thinking with a bit more order and structure. I find this useful because it is a visual way to guide a lot of thoughts into a linear order. It’s a forcing function to organize your thoughts into story of sorts.

The ability to link boards together allows you to layer a bit more complexity on top in a natural way. By associating my board of ‘Stuff’ with ‘Artificial Intelligence’ I’m saying that I’m allowing information to flow (one-way) in that direction. I only create these roads and lanes as needed and most of the actual text is placed in one text file. You can contrast this to a Zettlekasten approach where you are more motivated to create atomic notes and link them together. (I discuss pros and cons of this approach vs. Zettlekasten in a little more depth in the article about topics I linked to above.)

#### Embracing the #hashtag

I have had a sort-of love-hate relationship with hashtags. On one hand, they are essentially the unofficial standard to organically group notes, tweets, articles, etc. into topics. On the other hand, I think they can lead to a lot of noise if misused and backlinks are a very similar concept and bit more powerful.

This being said, if you have relatively few categories to work with, hashtags can be a useful and I thought hard about how I want to use them in Kanban Plus. The primary use case is in the ‘Copy to calendar’ feature.

In Kanban Plus, when you copy a card to a calendar that has a hashtag, the first hashtag that also matches a calendar name is the calendar the card is associated with. Additionally, the card background is now colored according the color defined in the ‘Full Calendar’ plugin.

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*u9UI2-fOCIf6kI16S9WFvw.png)

This is a simple but visually striking way to color your calendars and your lists. And the #hashtag is the common denominator. Above is an example where I’m going to copy a simple card to a calendar called ‘software’. The result is below. The card is associated with the hashtag ‘#software’ and I have a draggable event to the left on my calendar. They are both colored by the color of the calendar.

![](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*fG2H_tYqQJQgaap6FPZ7Cg.png)

### Core Principles of Kanban Plus

Taking a step back, I’d like to emphsize that Kanban Plus is not about just adding whatever feature I want. There are a few principles I think about when working on this plugin. Kanban Plus:

1.  thrives on being  **fast and local**. These two features are what I believe sets apart the Obsidian Kanban world spiritually from many other tempting Kanban software.
2.  **is based on a foundation of lists and calendars**  so the most useful features would center around simple and powerful uses of both together.
3.  **treats lists are multipurpose and multidimensional**: they can be used for tracking tasks, projects, organizing thoughts, structure writing, and more. Features in Kanban Plus embrace the type of variety of uses of lists like the ones I discussed above.
4.  is  **modular**. The features added to Kanban Plus can be used independently of each other but when used together the experience is greatly enhanced. Take the hashtag-based integration of cards with calendar names. Hastags for cards are independently useful, but when combined with their eponymous calendars, can be linked together by name and color. As another example, copying list items to other files in no way changes the core functionality of the Kanban plugin and doesn’t attempt to tie the two files together too rigidly. If the feature went away both boards will be just as useful as they were when integrated.

----------

**Finally, Kanban Plus is a true fork**  of the Kanban plugin and attempts to stay in sync with it. The beauty of the original plugin is how simple plain text lists turn instantly into Kanban boards and I have yet to find another plugin that does this so elegantly and true to a simple plain text format.

I decided after some thought to simply keep a fork because the original Kanban plugin seems a bit out of maintenance. The original Kanban Plugin still has many open pull requests and a number of discussions online about alternatives to this plugin ([eg](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-kanban-view/101593)).

Kanban Plus aims to use AI tooling to the extent reasonable fix issues with the plugin and add useful features. This way, the plugin is less likely to be neglected because smaller fixes and features should be easy to manage.

My hope is  **not**  that this fork replaces or becomes the new official Kanban standard in the Obsidian world. For example, you can imagine a world where Kanban Plus works well with Obsidian Bases or that  [Bases embraces some form of ordered lists](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/tech-habits-lists-in-obsidian-kanban-vs-obsidian-bases-f613b1673d56). However, in the absence of any tool that works better for me I do hope, as mentioned before, that this tool can be useful to at least one more person and that the ideas behind the tool may find additional homes.

Thanks in advance to anyone who joins me on this adventure. ‘Till next time! 🏔️

notes.ref: Tech Habits — How I Use Readwise to Think Better (Not Just Collect More)
notes.title: Tech Habits: How I Use Readwise to Think Better (Not Just Collect More)
notes.body:
# Tech Habits: How I Use Readwise to Think Better (Not Just Collect More)

#### For me, Readwise is not a highlights graveyard. Here’s how it earns its keep.

![1.00](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1024/1*G0qYZKHv6gGOs9CqtJ0yQg.png)

I’d wager that many people use Readwise like a highlights dump.

Readwise would like to have you believe that you read something interesting, you highlight it, and the app faithfully reminds you of these gems every morning through spaced repetition.

In reality, maybe you review the queue, maybe you don’t. Either way, the highlights accumulate. But the thinking doesn’t really change.

I used Readwise like that for a while. But I’ve come to believe the real power is both upstream and downstream of the highlights themselves. It’s about what you do with content around the time Readwise sees it, and what you do after you’ve read it.

This post is about that fuller picture. And it’s organized around a principle I keep coming back to from a previous installment on [Notable Folders](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/this-one-idea-changed-the-way-i-organized-my-notes-forever-f293371a0d5b): the well-organized mind isn’t about storing everything or bookkeeping. It’s about feeling at home in your own thinking.

In short, Readwise, its Reader app, and Obsidian integration serve as my primary tool to:

* capture full text for various types of media, notably YouTube videos

* consume and highlight this media

* whittle exports for use in a broader and more memorable context

A few things worth being clear about:

This is not a system that requires constant maintenance. The automation handles the routine. You handle the intention.

It’s not about capturing everything or building an overwhelming second brain. Most things you save don’t need to go further than the queue. That’s fine.

It’s not about replacing memory. Memory often does most of the work. The system exists for what memory drops.

For me, Readwise Reader functions as the capture layer for pretty much any content I want to consume later (eg. podcasts, articles, YouTube videos, PDFs, etc). It is also the primary place where I prefer to listen to what I’ve captured. If I feel compelled to highlight something, that’s a signal that I probably would benefit from re-reading this content later.

![1.00](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1024/1*Wb0GWuJBZPcB4U0Xw_7cvQ.png)

The piece that makes Readwise indispensable for me is the Obsidian integration. Critically, when you set it up, your highlights**and the full text** of saved articles automatically convert to markdown and land in your Obsidian vault with appropriate YAML front matter. Not the default, generic metadata, but intentional metadata you configure to match how your system already works.

![1.00](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1024/1*2-v-4ZC4tWMYlUof5sOocw.png)

The upshot is that everything I’ve highlighted lives locally in Obsidian. This means this content is satisfyingly portable, offline-accessible, and efficiently retrievable. This last point is underrated. Content disappears from the internet constantly. URLs rot. Paywalls go up. Newsletters die. With this integration, what I’ve highlighted doesn’t depend on the original page surviving. It’s mine now, in a format that any future tool can read.

A quick note before going further: Readwise and Obsidian are well-known complementary tools with an existing integration. I’m not describing a jerry-rigged workaround. The point isn’t the integration itself but how to use it intentionally rather than just letting it run in the background accumulating noise you never revisit.

Perhaps here’s where most Readwise users stop. But it’s also where the real work should begin.

For me, not everything that lands in Obsidian is equal. The funnel works like this:

1. Reader captures broadly,
2. you consume and highlight selectively,
3. Obsidian receives everything, and then
4. _you decide what actually matters._

I’ve found that memory alone works surprisingly well for most things. This fact alone challenges the need for a fancier system like this.

BUT memory has a ceiling. I often find that connections I’ve made that seemed obvious in the moment don’t survive the week.

Crucially, this system isn’t about capturing everything. It’s about having just enough structure to catch and sort what matters. I now almost gleefully accept that I’ll never read some articles I save, never highlight some articles I’ve read, and never further organize articles I’ve highlighted.

The articles that survive my whittling process are the ones that earn it through my own thought on how I want to value them, not because some automated system forced them into Obsidian. This is the subtle thing that separates a living system from a highlights graveyard.

In a previous Tech Habits post, I described ALPPS: Archives, Logs, People, Projects, Spaces. Within that structure live what I call Notable Folders, which are Wikipedia-style home bases for people, projects, and spaces that matter to me. Each notable folder is a main document with related notes and links accumulating over time.

When a Readwise highlight connects to someone I think about often, a thinker, an author, a colleague, I don’t just let it sit in the Obsidian vault. I place a link or a note directly in that person’s notable folder.

For example, I recently listened to a SOLVED podcast episode on boundaries. An intriguing note about the relationship between values and behavior caught my attention. Instead of leaving it in the general Readwise pile, I linked it into Mark Manson’s notable folder with a few comments and how it relates to a couple of other people of interest. Now that highlight isn’t a random tidbit from something I listened to once. It’s part of a growing body of thinking about how Manson (and team) approaches values and behavior, sitting alongside his books and other things I’ve noted over time.

This is the move that makes the system help me think. I am not auto-tagging everything or spending a crazy amount of effort creating atomic notes. They key is I am making a deliberate choice that this article or some content within it is stand-out _even among articles and highlights I found interesting_ , and I want it to live somewhere that will give it a longer life.

Notably (pun intended), the link to the content goes directly to my Obsidian note (which also has the full text of the podcast), **not** to the original podcast page . The page might disappear. My note won’t.

You may assume the payoff of a highlights system is better recall of facts or producing prolifically from compendium content that resonates.

But for me, the unexpectedly awesome payoff has been being more grounded in conversation with other people.

Because the highlight has been linked to a notable folder and contextualized within a broader body of my own thinking, it’s not just something I’ve just once read or some fun fact I’d mention at the water cooler.

It’s a piece of information couched comfortably in my home of thought. It has earned a place in a scaffold I’ve been cultivating for years. I know more intimately why it matters _to me_. I know how deeply how it connects to other topics of interest for me, and not in the “I’ve linked this to a thousand other notes” kinda way.

![1.00](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1024/1*o6DkTz2pskjNYAVbNsHAfA.png)

And as a bonus, in Obsidian, the Readwise notes render as clean local files. No loading, no app to open, and no dependency on the service being up. Often more pleasant and efficient to browse than Readwise itself!

Also, a previous Tech Habits post on [standards versus interfaces](https://medium.com/@geetduggal/tech-habits-ditching-digital-capture-and-why-claude-hasn-t-killed-obsidian-for-me-3f912b569b37) is worth connecting here. Here you can think of Markdown as the standard underneath both tools. Readwise and Obsidian are interfaces that conceptually sit on top of it. If either tool disappeared tomorrow, the notes remain intact and readable in any text editor.

Finally, a few things on a slow burn that I’ll write about soon 😀:

* **Multiscale journaling** : a workflow where voice capture flows through AI synthesis to hand-written artifacts and eventually public graduation, the most powerful piece in the pipeline and the one I’m most excited about

* **The pocket e-reader as personal library** : carrying your own writing, key texts, and saved notes on eInk in a way that changes how you read your own thinking

* **The shutdown ritual** : a simple trick for how I close my day

* **Collections in bullet journals mapped to people, projects, and spaces** : how physical and digital notable folders talk to each other

* **From private notes to public GitHub and a personal website** : the architecture of graduating your thinking from flat local files to a multi-channel publishing setup with near-zero additional friction

Till next time! 🏔️

![1.00](https://medium.com/_/stat?event=post.clientViewed\&referrerSource=full_rss\&postId=3d955e2983fa)
